LinkedAlliance Hub · Onboarding Presentation

What I Learned

A deep-dive into how the Hub is built, secured, and shipped

Akshar Dharwadkar  ·  June 26, 2026
Big Picture

The LinkedAlliance Hub

A multi-tenant SaaS operating system for the Linked Accounting Alliance — a network of independent CPA and accounting firms. One platform. Shared infrastructure. Private firm data.

12
Capability domains
10
RBAC roles
6
Role desks
~50
MCP tools exposed
What it replaces
  • Siloed CRM, project tools, and docs
  • Manual compliance and referral tracking
  • Disconnected help-desk & service delivery
  • Spreadsheet-based reporting and pipeline
What it enables
  • CRM, tasks, KB, tickets — one login
  • AI-assisted workflows across all desks
  • Claude access via MCP (OAuth 2.1)
  • Admin-managed RBAC without code changes
Architecture

Tech stack

LayerTechnologyNotes
FrontendReact 18 + React Router v6 + TypeScriptSPA compiled to dist/
BuildVite 5 + SWCDev server localhost:8080
UIshadcn/ui (Radix UI) + Tailwind CSS 3.4Path alias @/ → src/
Server stateTanStack React Query v5useQuery / useMutation throughout
BackendSupabase — Postgres + PostgREST + Auth + Storage + Edge FunctionsThe entire API surface
Rich textTipTapKB articles, check-in responses
ChartsRecharts · D3-geo · LeafletAnalytics, maps on BDR/SDR desks
DeployVercelPreview per branch · production on main
One thing to know: src/integrations/supabase/client.ts and types.ts are auto-generated — never edit them. Use db from src/lib/db.ts when the generated types lag the schema.
Architecture

What happens when you click something

Browser React component
→
useQuery /
useMutation React Query
→
Supabase client Auto-generated SDK
→
PostgREST HTTP → SQL
→
Postgres + RLS Authorization runs here
Key insight: Row-Level Security policies run inside the database. There is no middleware layer. Every query Supabase executes passes through RLS — the React app is never fully trusted.
Edge Functions (Supabase)
Server-side logic lives in supabase/functions/ — integrations, AI calls, OAuth, webhooks. Called via Vercel rewrites or direct invocation.
No app server
React SPA served statically from Vercel CDN. All compute is either Supabase (DB, edge fns) or the browser.
Codebase

Where things live

linkedalliance/ ├── src/ │ ├── pages/ route-level components │ ├── components/ │ │ ├── desks/<slug>/ role desks │ │ ├── admin/ Admin.tsx (98 KB — careful) │ │ └── ui/ shadcn primitives │ ├── hooks/ useAuth, usePermissions… │ ├── lib/ │ │ ├── db.ts ← use this, not client.ts │ │ └── activityLogger.ts │ └── integrations/supabase/ │ ├── client.ts DO NOT EDIT │ └── types.ts DO NOT EDIT (310 KB) ├── supabase/ │ ├── migrations/ live ordered schema history │ └── functions/ edge functions ├── docs/ legacy hand-run SQL (historical) └── scripts/ check-supabase-security.mjs
App.tsx — flat route config. All authenticated pages render inside <PortalLayout> which mounts the sidebar, impersonation/demo banners.
Two migration tracks:
supabase/migrations/*.sql — live, timestamp-ordered, managed by Supabase CLI

docs/*.sql — legacy, hand-run in Supabase SQL Editor. Historical reference only. New changes always go in migrations/.
Large files need care: Admin.tsx (98 KB), MyCheckIns.tsx (59 KB), Work.tsx (56 KB). Extract components when making substantial edits.
Database

How data is organized

DomainKey tables
Identityprofiles, custom_roles, user_role_assignments
Projectsprojects, project_members, tasks, subtasks
Ticketstickets, ticket_messages
Documentsdocuments, folder hierarchy, Storage
Desksbdr_*, sdr_*, expansion_opportunities, ssg_*
Auth / MCPoauth_clients, oauth_access_tokens, email_connections
Platformactivity_logs, notifications, custom_field_definitions
Marketingfirm_candidates (staging) → organizations (live)
Migration track — live
supabase/migrations/*.sql
~60 timestamp-ordered files. This IS the schema. Applied via Supabase CLI (supabase db push).
Migration track — legacy
docs/*.sql
~50 historical files run manually in the SQL Editor. Pre-dates the migrations convention. Read-only for reference.
One rule: never make schema changes directly in the Supabase dashboard. Everything goes through a migrations/ file so history is traceable.
Security

How firms stay separated

Four layers enforce firm isolation. Each one independently blocks access — they compound.

1
Active-user gate — is_active_user(auth.uid()) on every READ policy. Pending or inactive users get nothing.
2
Org scoping — resources carry an org_id. Policies join on the user's org. One firm's rows are invisible to another.
3
RESTRICTIVE block-pending policy — AND-ed over every command on every table. Even if a permissive policy passes, a pending user cannot proceed.
4
Document tiers — view_alliance / view_org / view_private. Explicit grants for private visibility.
-- present on EVERY significant table CREATE POLICY "Block pending users" ON public.tasks AS RESTRICTIVE FOR ALL TO authenticated USING (is_active_user(auth.uid())) WITH CHECK (is_active_user(auth.uid()));
RESTRICTIVE = AND, not OR. Postgres OR-folds all PERMISSIVE policies, then AND-folds all RESTRICTIVE ones. A RESTRICTIVE policy that fails always blocks the operation — it cannot be "overridden" by any permissive policy.
Auth

How users get in

Login methods
  • Email + password (Supabase Auth)
  • Google SSO (provider = 'google')
  • Microsoft SSO (provider = 'microsoft')
Invite → onboarding flow
1. Admin creates user → invite email sent
↓
2. User clicks link → /claim?token=
↓
3. /setup-account — sets password
↓
4. status = "pending" → PendingApproval screen
↓
5. Admin approves → status = "active" → full access
Session gating in App.tsx
ConditionRenders
No sessionAuth — login page
Session + status="pending"PendingApproval
Session + activeFull app via ImpersonationAwareRoutes
Impersonation: admins can act as any user. Real identity preserved for audit. All activity written during impersonation records the real actor. Warm banner shown. Code: useImpersonation.tsx
Demo mode: useDemoMode() replaces real names/emails/phones with synthetic placeholders. Useful for screenshots or demos.
Permissions

Custom RBAC model

Three tables
custom_roles name, color, is_system role_permissions role_id → custom_roles permission_key (text) user_role_assignments user_id → profiles UNIQUE(user_id) — one role per user
Permission key format: resource.action
Examples: tasks.create · projects.view_all · mcp.write

RPC get_user_permissions(uid) returns all keys, or ["*"] if the role has is_system = true.
RoleTierNotes
Adminis_systemGets "*" — all permissions
ExecutivesBroadAll admin.* + mcp.*
Board MemberBroadRead-wide + mcp.read only
PartnerStandardProjects + tasks + mcp.write
ManagerStandardPartner + task delete + performance
MemberDefaultStandard access + mcp.write
SSG MemberDefaultMember + ticket mgmt + SSG tools
Content EditorStandardFull KB/announcements write
Read OnlyLimitedNo MCP access
ExternalMinimaldocuments.view_alliance only
Security

Row-Level Security deep dive

RLS enforces access at the PostgreSQL layer — every query is filtered server-side before data reaches the client. UI gates (usePermission, <PermissionGate>) are advisory shortcuts; RLS is the real security boundary that cannot be bypassed by a crafted request or a missing UI check.

Consistent pattern on every table
-- Read: active users only USING (is_active_user(auth.uid())) -- Write: admin or specific permission WITH CHECK (has_role(auth.uid(), 'admin'::app_role)) -- Hard gate: AND-ed over ALL commands CREATE POLICY "Block pending users" AS RESTRICTIVE FOR ALL TO authenticated USING (is_active_user(auth.uid())) WITH CHECK (is_active_user(auth.uid()));
Legacy RBAC still backs RLS helpers. is_active_user, has_role, has_permission come from an earlier FK-based RBAC (tables: roles, permissions). These are still present and used by RLS policies — not yet dropped.
Helper functions
FunctionWhat it checks
is_active_user(uid)profiles.status = 'active'
has_role(uid, role)Legacy app_role enum check
has_permission(uid, key)Legacy permissions table
Client-side enforcement:
usePermission("resource.action") → checks resolved permission array or "*" wildcard. Returns false while loading (fail-closed). The component gate is advisory — the DB is the real boundary.
Two RBAC systems, one RLS layer. The custom RBAC (user_role_assignments + role_permissions with string permission keys) is what useAuth / usePermissions query at runtime — it drives role desks, UI gates, and the admin console. But the RLS helper functions (is_active_user, has_role, has_permission) still call into the legacy FK-based tables (roles, app_role enum). Both must stay in sync until the legacy tables are formally dropped. At migration rollout, old roles were mapped: legacy Admin → Admin, Member → Member, Viewer → Read Only.
Permissions

Permission enforcement end-to-end

Defined
Admin adds key to role
RolesAdminTab.tsx
→
Granted
Admin assigns role to user
user_role_assignments
→
Resolved
get_user_permissions RPC
returns keys or ["*"]
→
UI gate
usePermission() / PermissionGate
advisory — can be bypassed
→
DB gate (RLS)
Real authority — always enforced
USING / WITH CHECK
// hooks/usePermissions.tsx:88-92 export function usePermission(key: string) { const { permissions, loading } = useContext(PermissionsContext); if (loading) return false; // fail-closed return permissions.includes("*") || permissions.includes(key); }
<PermissionGate> — thin wrapper over usePermission. Guards UI elements. Used consistently for nav items, action buttons, admin tabs.
Not every button is gated. Some actions (like creating a task) rely solely on RLS. The permission key may be defined in the RBAC catalog but not yet enforced at the UI layer.
Security

File uploads & sensitive data security

Document visibility tiers
view_alliance
All active members. Default sharing level.
view_org
Scoped to the uploader's org. Other firms can't see.
view_private
Owner + explicit grants only. Computed by documentPermissions.ts
Storage security
  • Supabase Storage + Storage RLS policies
  • Policies defined in docs/fix-storage-policies.sql
  • Recycle bin (soft delete) + restore flow
  • W9 uploads isolated by separate migration
OAuth tokens & credentials
  • Google/Microsoft OAuth tokens encrypted in email_connections table
  • Encrypted/decrypted only in supabase/functions/_shared/gmail-crypto.ts
  • Tokens never exposed to the browser
  • MCP access tokens stored as SHA-256 hash in oauth_access_tokens
  • Personal MCP tokens hashed in profiles.mcp_api_token

Admin
RBAC, users, integrations, MCP connections, audit log, desks
Product Tour

The 6 Desks — role-specific daily workspaces

Two-level gate: user needs desks.access and the per-desk key (e.g. desks.bdr). Admin manages desk slugs in the desks table.
BDR
desks.bdr
Business Development Reps
Outbound prospecting. BDRs claim batches of ~10 prospects, action each (claim / skip / flag). Includes map view and enrichment dashboard.
SDR
desks.sdr
Sales Development Reps
Firm prospecting. Most complex desk — the canonical reference implementation. Queues of CPA firms; research and draft outreach. "View As" for managers.
Client Expansion
desks.client-expansion
Expansion Advisors
AI-scored upsell/cross-sell opportunities. CFO Advisory focus. Tier 1–3 ranking, full sales funnel from Not Contacted to Converted.
Daily Prepper
desks.daily-prepper
Any role
Pre-meeting briefing. Google Calendar meetings enriched with CRM context and Claude AI-generated meeting briefs. Requires calendar.readonly scope.
SSG Engagements
desks.ssg-engagements
SSG Advisors
Full advisory engagement management (~5,100 lines). Meetings, emails, tasks, transcripts, AI signals. Most integrations (Gmail, Calendar, Fathom, Claude).
Marketing Website
desks.marketing-website
Design / Marketing
Firm intake → scraper → SQL generation → site preview pipeline for the marketing site. Sandbox Supabase; builder can't touch production.
Roadmap

What still feels unfinished

  • Microsoft email path — email_connections table recognizes provider = 'microsoft' but the OAuth flow is not fully implemented. Google is the active path.
  • Legacy RBAC tables — roles, permissions (FK-based) still in the DB backing RLS helpers. The custom RBAC is live, but the legacy tables haven't been dropped yet.
  • Marketing Website desk — staging → live promotion pipeline is built, but Traffic & SEO tab is a "Coming Soon" placeholder. Site launch pending.
  • CreateTaskDialog.tsx — a full "Create Task" dialog component exists at src/components/tasks/ but is never imported or rendered. Dead code.
  • 9-box scores are immutable by policy — nine_box_scores has no UPDATE or DELETE RLS policy. Whether intentional (immutable record) or an oversight is unclear.
  • Two migration tracks — having both supabase/migrations/ and docs/*.sql creates ambiguity about the definitive schema. Full migration of all docs SQL into versioned migrations would help.
Integrations

What connects to the Hub

SystemDirectionWhat flowsUsed by
HubSpot in Deal pipeline, engagements, contact ownership Wealth reporting, Reporting dashboards, Daily Prepper
Google Gmail in+out Email threads read in; drafts created in Gmail Drafts SSG Engagements, Client Expansion
Google Calendar in Calendar events → prep_calendar_events, ssg_calendar_events Daily Prepper, SSG Engagements
Fathom in Call transcripts via webhook → ssg_meetings → Claude signals SSG Engagements
Ninety.io both EOS tasks sync bidirectionally. _sync_lock prevents loops. Projects & Tasks (synced tasks)
Gemini (Google) local parse .docx exports from Google Meet AI notes — parsed in browser, no API call SSG Engagements (UploadGeminiNotesDialog)
Canopy import CPA practice management contacts; external import, no live API SSG Engagements (contact source)
All real integration logic lives in Supabase Edge Functions (supabase/functions/). Client-side code is thin — it builds URLs, reads connection status, or triggers edge functions. Tokens are encrypted server-side and never returned to the browser.
AI

Two different Claudes

Claude — inside the product
AI features that end users experience
  • SSG Engagements — ssg-engagement-ask (Q&A over transcripts + emails), ssg-extract-signals, ssg-portfolio-ask
  • Client Expansion — AI opportunity scoring, fit summaries
  • Daily Prepper — AI meeting briefs via MeetingBrief component
  • BDR / SDR — AI email draft generation
Hub-as-MCP-server: The Hub acts as an OAuth 2.1 Authorization Server + MCP server (hub-oauth + hub-mcp edge functions). Exposes ~50 tools (tasks, projects, desk data). Claude Desktop or Claude.ai can connect directly to the Hub on behalf of a logged-in member.
Claude Code — the build tool
How we actually build the Hub
  • Reads the codebase, finds patterns, answers questions
  • Edits files, creates components, writes migrations
  • Guided by CLAUDE.md files (root + per-desk)
  • Operates within guardrails (CODEOWNERS, sandbox DB, SPEC.md)
  • Used by every builder — James, Jacob, Trey, and now me
Important distinction: Claude AI calling the Hub's MCP tools = product feature. Claude Code editing the Hub's source code = build workflow. They use different auth paths and have completely different permissions.
Shipping

From idea to production

Branch
feature/* fix/* desk-*
no pushing to main directly
→
Pull Request
fill PR template
pick tier 1/2/3
→
Code-owner review
@jprince1985 or @jacoblinkedalliance
required by branch protection
→
Vercel preview
auto-built per branch
test here before merging
→
Merge → production
Vercel auto-deploys main
release-notes.yml records it
No pre-merge CI. There are no GitHub Actions that run lint, tests, or build on pull requests. Those checks are manual: run npm run lint, npm run test, npm run build, and tick the PR checklist. The only workflow that exists runs after merge to record the change.
release-notes.yml runs on push to main. Classifies the push as reviewed (via PR) or bypass (direct push). Records a tiered entry to the Hub via record-release edge function. Pings both owners via webhook if a bypass is detected.
Shipping

PR tier system

Tier 1 Frontend-only
No DB schema changes. No RLS changes. No edge function changes. No external integration changes.
Includes: UI, copy, component logic, styling
Rollback: Vercel rollback only. No notes needed.
Tier 2 Additive backend
Adds new tables, columns, RLS policies, or edge functions. Nothing existing is modified or removed.
Old code paths still work without the new additions.
Rollback: Vercel reverts frontend. New schema remains in place, unused, until removed in a follow-up PR.
Tier 3 Destructive
Drops/renames columns, tightens RLS, modifies edge functions non-additively, backfills data, changes external integrations.
Required: pre-deploy Supabase snapshot ID, snapshot timestamp, rollback steps, estimated data-loss window.
Rule: "Classify up when in doubt." Tier no longer gates who can approve (since v3.0, 2026-06-01) — but tier drives the rollback planning, which is the whole point.
Database

Changing the schema safely

The process
1
Create a migration file in supabase/migrations/ with a timestamp prefix. Never touch the Supabase dashboard schema editor.
2
Run the security audit
node scripts/check-supabase-security.mjs
Calls Supabase security advisor API. Exits 1 on ERROR lints. Run after any DDL change.
3
PR + tier classification. Tier 2 if additive, Tier 3 if destructive. Fill rollback section accordingly.
4
Builders without Supabase access queue requests in SPEC.md. Jacob applies the change.
What can go wrong
  • Dashboard edit — bypasses migration history. Schema drifts from what's in supabase/migrations/. Next CLI deploy may overwrite or conflict.
  • Tier 3 without snapshot — if a destructive migration fails mid-way and there's no snapshot, data loss has no recovery path.
  • RLS regression — tightening a policy locks out legitimate users. Loosening it leaks data. The advisor script catches obvious gaps, not logic errors.
  • verify_jwt reset — several edge functions run with verify_jwt = false and self-authenticate. A CLI redeploy used to silently reset this to true, breaking the function. Now pinned in supabase/config.toml.
Claude Code

How Claude Code works here

Root CLAUDE.md guardrails
  • DO NOT EDIT src/integrations/supabase/client.ts or types.ts — auto-generated
  • When types lag schema, use db from src/lib/db.ts (typed as any)
  • TypeScript strict mode is OFF — don't fight the types
  • Large files (Admin.tsx 98 KB, etc.) — extract components rather than editing inline
  • Call logActivity() for any significant user action
Marketing desk — isolation model (concrete example)
ConstraintHow enforced
Code isolationCODEOWNERS blocks merges touching files outside desks/marketing-website/
DB accessBuilder has no Supabase access. Uses sandbox project. All DB changes queued in SPEC.md → Jacob applies
Go-livePR from desk-marketing-website → main; Jacob reviews
Any builder can read any file in the repo freely. The guardrails govern writing and merging, not reading. Claude Code follows CLAUDE.md instructions and CODEOWNERS enforces the boundary at the PR gate.
Deep Dive

Tracing task creation end-to-end

New Task button
Tasks.tsx:1215 — onClick=handleCreateNewTask
⚠ No UI permission gate
tasks.create defined RolesAdminTab.tsx:69 but never checked here
usePermission() is NOT called before this action
db.insert("tasks")
Tasks.tsx:681 — sets assigned_by = effectiveUserId (required by RLS)
RLS INSERT — real authorization
docs/tasks-migration.sql:96 + migration 20260609...:262
WITH CHECK: is_active_user AND assigned_by = auth.uid()
public.tasks
Row written. RLS was the only gate that mattered.
The incident: "block pending users" policy was PERMISSIVE (the default). Postgres OR-folded it with every other policy — collapsing all task access to just is_active_user(). Any active user could read, write, or delete every task.
-- ❌ Before (insecure — PERMISSIVE default) CREATE POLICY "Block pending users" ON public.tasks USING (is_active_user(auth.uid())); -- Postgres OR-ed this with everything ↑ -- ✅ After (migration 20260609120000) CREATE POLICY "Block pending users tasks" ON public.tasks AS RESTRICTIVE FOR ALL TO authenticated USING (is_active_user(auth.uid())) WITH CHECK (is_active_user(auth.uid()));
Live Demo

Claude Code — live

The query
Trace how creating a new task is authorized in this codebase. Start from the UI button and follow it all the way to the database.
Expected outcome: Claude finds the New Task button (Tasks.tsx:1215), notes there is no usePermission() call, follows the db.insert to the RLS INSERT policy, and identifies the RESTRICTIVE block-pending gate.
What to narrate while Claude works
  • Claude is reading the codebase in real time — no pre-loaded context
  • Watch for it to grep for usePermission near the button and find nothing
  • It should surface the RLS INSERT policy as the real gate
  • The answer demonstrates that the Hub's security model is DB-first, not component-first
Backup prompt if it wanders:
"Where is tasks.create enforced?" or "Show me the RLS policies on the tasks table."
Next Steps

How I'd build my first feature

Feature: Hub AI Chat — a chat widget trained on the Hub's own KB, announcements, and docs. Users ask questions; Claude answers with context pulled from real Hub content.

Tier 2 — fully additive. New tables, new edge function, new component. Nothing existing touched.
1
Migration in supabase/migrations/ — ai_chat_sessions + ai_chat_messages tables.
RLS: users read/write their own sessions only. Run check-supabase-security.mjs after DDL.
2
Edge function hub-chat — on each message: search knowledge_articles + announcements → inject top-N chunks as context → stream Claude response.
Follows ssg-engagement-ask pattern exactly — already in the codebase.
3
Permission key ai.chat added to catalog in RolesAdminTab.tsx. Granted to Member / Manager / Partner / SSG Member. Read Only gets nothing.
4
UI widget AiChatWidget — floating button or sidebar panel. Gate with usePermission("ai.chat"). Streams tokens from edge function; history stored in ai_chat_messages.
5
Open PR — Tier 2. Schema section: new tables only, nothing existing modified. Rollback: Vercel reverts frontend; tables remain in place, unused.
6
Verify on Vercel preview — Member sees chat widget, Read Only does not. KB content surfaces in answers. One code-owner approval → merge → prod.
Ties the whole deck together: migration (slide 20) → permission key (slide 9) → usePermission UI gate (slide 11) → RLS on chat tables (slide 10) → edge function follows existing Claude pattern (slide 17) → Tier 2 PR (slide 19).
01 / 25
